From the heart, truly ...

Yes, I called out Narayana, Neuland, and Laurus correctly. I am grateful for that. But please understand something important. Just because I got those right does not mean I will identify the next big winner.

Markets do not reward past accuracy with future certainty. Every new call is a fresh decision in a fresh environment. It is like a coin toss. Even if someone guessed correctly five times in a row, that streak tells you nothing about the very next flip.

I do not want admiration built on a streak. I want trust built on realism.

So please align your expectations. Appreciate the wins, yes. But do not assume they guarantee the next one. I will always do the work sincerely. I will always share with honesty. But certainty is something none of us truly have.

Let us stay grounded.

R to @OurWorldInData: On our new Work and Employment topic page, we bring together the key labor market indicators — from unemployment rates and participation, to informality and much more — and explain what they mean in clear terms and with helpful diagrams.

→ https://ourworldindata.org/work-employment

R to @OurWorldInData: That means the unemployment rate can rise not only when people lose jobs — but also when more people enter the labor force by starting to look for a job, but haven’t found one yet.

R to @OurWorldInData: What’s the “labor force”? It’s the sum of everyone employed + unemployed.

The unemployment rate is the number of unemployed people divided by the total labor force.

R to @OurWorldInData: In labor statistics, a person is counted as unemployed if they:
– Don’t have a job
– Are actively looking for one
– Can start soon

If someone doesn’t have a job *but isn’t looking for one* — e.g., because they're studying, caring for family, or retired — they’re not unemployed, but outside the “labor force” entirely.

Being unemployed isn’t the same as not having a job — at least when it comes to labor statistics.

The map shows unemployment rates around the world in 2025.

A genuine advice to those looking to learn building w/ AI. Keep vibe coding for later part. Anyone can get access to vibe coding tools. Instead, start with a simple project building in VScode, learn how to install python, packages if you don't know already. Learn modularization/componentization methods as you build. Learn about various libariries & API integrations. Learn versioning & Git integration. Instead of directly heading to vibe coding tools, connect your coding tools with VScode. This will give you much needed fundamental knowledge of software building. Rather than chasing flashy goals of deploying something & tweeting about it, focus on things that matter. That's the way to build sustainable future.

There are some good R&D based businesses in India, but we need more firms to dream big and take risks

However, the problem is not financial but cultural and perception-based

Many companies do capex in futuristic sectors, but then I see that brokerages are the first ones to call it a negative

I feel that is a very short-term and biased view - We need to change our mindset towards R&D, capex, and failure - Not every R&D is going to be successful, and it is also wrong to expect quick outcomes 

- The Kavach system by HBL took 20 years
- India began cryogenic engine efforts in the early 1990s after technology denial regimes blocked access. First successful indigenous cryogenic flight: 2014 (GSLV-D5). 
That’s ~20+ years again
- Jio was in network build + investment (burn) mode for ~6 years before full commercial monetization + ~4–5 years stabilization.
- L&T invested for decades to build heavy engineering & nuclear capability that few can replicate today.
- The Tejas program took ~30+ years from concept to scale orders.
- Bharat Forge - ATAGS (Advanced Towed Artillery Gun System) - R&D participation began: ~2013, Successful user trials & order movement: ~2022–23
- BHEL - Supercritical Thermal Power Equipment -Indigenous capability push: Early 2000s - Large-scale domestic deployment: 2012 onward; Gestation: ~8–12 years

Only when we normalize this will we truly focus on genuine R&D instead of settling for jugaadu innovation.

The bigger problem is that we’ve created a culture where not investing in R&D has become normal. Keeping the ship steady these days is not a positive sign; it should be taken as a firm whose management is lazy and is happy with the norm!

Companies prefer to play it safe, and then the same brokerages criticize them for not investing in futuristic sectors

No reco on stock names taken, just mentioned as examples

Oil shock watch: Brent Crude spiked on #IranWar fears.

Scenario math:
* Limited retaliation: +$5–10
* Direct hit on Iranian oil: +$10–12
* Hormuz disruption: >$90
* Regional war: >$100

With ~20% of global oil & >40% of India’s crude imports flowing via Hormuz, exposure is material. 

Key unknown: probability + duration of any Strait disruption.

Macro transmission is clear → higher crude = higher inflation → higher bond yields → compressed equity multiples.

Rule of thumb:
* Every $1 rise in crude adds ~$2bn to India’s annual import bill
* Every 10% oil spike → +30bps inflation, −15bps growth
* Every $10/bbl rise → ~$18bn CAD hit (~0.5% of GDP), pressuring INR & liquidity

Data Credits: Antique & JM Financial

Good read by JM Financial on Strait of Hormuz -

Crude remains the key macro variable for Indian equities under the current escalation scenario

-Likelihood of disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, through which nearly 20% of global oil flows and over 40% of India’s crude

-Brent has already moved to a 7- month high of ~USD 72.8/bbl, and scenario analysis suggests that Hormuz disruption could push prices above USD 90/bbl,

-For India, the impact is direct: every USD 1 rise in crude increases the annual import bill by ~USD 2bn, putting pressure on the trade balance

-Media reports indicate disruption to shipping activity in the Strait of Hormuz; confirmation of a complete and sustained closure remains unclear, making the probability and duration of supply interruption the key variable for markets.

-Markets are likely to move from earnings-driven to oil-driven trading in the near term. 

-Upstream energy and defence may see relative support, while oil-sensitive sectors such as OMCs, paints, tyres, aviation, and chemicals face margin pressure.

In 2026, focus on what you truly understand. Spend your time and energy on areas where you have specific knowledge and insight. At the same time, look for parts of the market that other people are ignoring.

Pay attention to opportunities that are not getting much attention. These may be companies or ideas that are under-owned or underappreciated. They might not be popular right now, but they could have strong potential. This is especially true when the company’s management team is honest, capable, and invested in the company’s long-term success.

The decisions you make today can have a big impact on where you are 5 years from now. Thinking long term and acting carefully now can put you in a much stronger position in the future.

RT by @srajagopalan: I’m not a lawyer so I won't interpret contracts for you. But I am an economist, so here's my take on the economics of what happened yesterday (TL;DR: Jesse is right).

One of the main drivers of economic activity in developed countries is a favorable regulatory environment and *certainty* about that environment. Any investment is risky, and regulatory/institutional uncertainty keeps companies from investing, growing, and building in these types of environments. Companies don't want to build a new plant if they think the govt will seize it or put extra regs on its products.

As an example, take the tariff bonanza of the past year +. It's one thing to enact a 15% across the board tariff and stick with it, and entirely another to draw up completely random numbers, change them constantly (e.g., because you don't like a commercial), all under the spectre of them being illegal in the first place (they were). The outcome was exactly as predicted: consumers paid most of the cost, manufacturing is shedding workers and pulling back on construction (after 4 years of increases). The economy does not like uncertainty.

The one bright shining star of the economy was AI. The US had two of the frontier labs and the most of the major tech companies. AI had the most favorable regulatory environment and was booming; investment was going through the roof. 

And then the secretary of defense decided to nuke the business model of one of those companies because of too many stupid reasons to list here. It would be perfectly fine to cancel their contract, but designating Anthropic a *supply chain risk* --which has so far been reserved for foreign adversaries -- cuts off much of their business. 

But so what? Anthropic is important, but it's just one company. The problem is that this overturns the regulatory certainty that has made the US such a beacon for AI. AI companies, which have a lot of options, will now think twice unless a capricious member of the admin wants to designate an important part of their supply chain risk.

Maybe this will all blow over, maybe there will be a walk back. But trust is hard to build, but very easy to break---especially when it comes to economics and risk. I hope I'm wrong.

Explore more charts on oil spills on our dedicated topic page:

Oil spills from tankers have fallen by more than 90% since the 1970s—

In the 1970s, oil spills from tankers — container ships transporting oil — were common. Between 70 and 100 spills occurred per year. That’s one or two spills every week.

This number has fallen by more than 90% since then. In the last decade, no year has had more than ten oil spills, as shown in the chart.

The *quantity* of oil spilled from tankers has also fallen dramatically. Over the last decade, the average is less than 10,000 tonnes per year, compared to over 300,000 tonnes in the 1970s.

(This Data Insight was written by @_HannahRitchie.)

In the short term, small companies can and will probably be hurt by volatility and disorder. Big swings in geopolitics or sudden escalation can create chaos, and smaller companies are often more vulnerable.

However, according to Nassim Taleb, small companies can actually benefit from these challenges over the long run. They are "antifragile", which means they can learn, pivot and ultimately grow stronger when faced with stress or uncertainty. Of course, you need patience (time), as major pivots never take a few weeks or months but years. Self-awareness and the capacity to suffer for years are not easy. Most can't. That's why most fail.

Second, that doesn’t mean you should invest in every small company you see. Careful selection is important. In my own experience, the best investments have been in companies that were not well-known or were overlooked by most investors.

Always prefer micro/smaller companies for wealth creation over 20 years.

Interesting stats by Monarch -

Indian equities have undergone a meaningful time & value correction over the last 18 months since Sep'24 - but the headline indices do not fully reflect the underlying damage

On the surface, benchmark indices are down only 4-5% from their highs, and the Nifty Smallcap index has corrected around 14%. However, beneath the surface, the broader market tells a very different story

The correction has been widespread across the listed universe.

Among companies with more than 1000 Cr MCAP
78% are down 20%+ from their all-time highs
64% are down 30%+
48% are down 40%+

Approximately 80% of stocks in the universe of over 1000 Cr are effectively in bear market territory. If companies below 1000 Cr market cap were included, the breadth would appear even weaker

Over the last few months, a narrow set of stocks has driven index performance.

Putting these pieces together -
- Market breadth is oversold
- Valuations in large parts of SMIDs have reset meaningfully

If history is any guide, phases of widespread correction combined with a completed or near-completed rate cut cycle have often laid the foundation for the next leg of recovery in SMIDs

While index levels may suggest stability, the broader market has already gone through a significant cleansing phase - and that is usually how the base for the next cycle is formed

R to @soicfinance: Few common things:

1. Started with reasonable guidance, and kept upgrading as results came.

2. Climbed up the value chain by changing the product mix. 

3. Unique businesses or a moat attached to them.

4. Margins kept going higher, and cos grew faster than the industry growth rates.

These are just examples- one can use the mental model of product mix change to find multiple other opportunities

It is important to separate price action from business performance...

Many stocks are not doing well in terms of price action - but that does not automatically imply weak businesses.

In many cases, earnings growth has remained healthy, yet stock prices are down.

Let us understand this with a simple practical example

- Stock price is 3000
- EPS is 100
- So P/E = 30x

Now EPS grows 20% YoY
New EPS = 120

At the same time, stock price corrects 25%
New stock price = 2250

Now what is the new P/E?
2250 divided by 120 = 18.75x

So despite earnings increasing, the multiple compresses from 30x to 18.75x.

This is how valuations reset during time & price corrections. That reset is what creates a more favourable risk-reward setup.

As long as earnings growth & visibility remain intact, there is no reason to exit simply because a stock is not moving.

Exiting only because the share price is not moving reflects impatience rather than process. Markets do not reward activity - they reward conviction.

It makes sense to switch only if a better opportunity is available or if the original thesis is no longer valid.

Markets move through sector rotations. One sector comes into favour, while another moves out of favour.

If one keeps chasing whichever sector is currently in momentum, the approach gradually shifts from investing to trading. In that case, strict stop-loss discipline becomes essential.

In the SMID universe, fundamentals alone do not trigger re-rating. Liquidity & sentiment play a very important role. A company can continue to deliver 20% earnings growth and still remain cheap for an extended period.

Re-rating typically begins when liquidity improves & sentiment turns positive. That is when multiples expand again.

If fundamentals are deteriorating, that is a different discussion altogether. But where earnings continue to compound & valuations have cooled off due to time correction, the setup gradually turns favourable.

This is a phase to hunt for bottom-up opportunities - positioning today to participate meaningfully when the next broader upcycle begins.

This is simply brilliant! Enterprise software can consume prompts much efficiently when exposed via prompt registry which in turn can be consumed in a development co-pilot. I am sure we will have strategic & specialist prompt engineering roles coming up ver

While its fancy to vibe code & talk about an army of agents doing things, it's far from a sophisticated multi-agent system. It's more of a fragmented pattern. In traditional software, we have Microservices Vs. Monoliths. Today, it's micro-agents. While it's fair to categorize systems into Orchestrators, Planners and Action agents, the subtle difference is lost when you try for over-granularity. When you have too many agents, you run a risk of losing distinct internal states. You dilute context, you waste compute. 

Focus on context injection, adaptive tool discovery, recursive reasoning, leading to a design with fewer reasoning-dense agents. The future of AI design is not more agents, but more capable agents with better environmental awareness. Focus on maximizing compute per token. 

The real deal is to design a single agent capable of adaptive internal role-shifting.

R to @OurWorldInData: Max previously wrote about why deforestation is happening, and what we can do to bring the long history of deforestation to an end: https://ourworldindata.org/global-forest-transition

Deforestation is no longer inevitable—

In the past, forests around the world were cut down on a massive scale. We lost some of the world’s richest ecosystems.

In recent decades, the picture has become more complex. Deforestation has not ended, but it is no longer happening everywhere.

Since 1990, some regions have continued to lose large areas of forest, while others have slowed this long-run trend — and even reversed it.

The map shows regional changes in forest area based on the latest data from the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations.

Deforestation has been particularly large in South America and Africa. At the same time, the forested area has expanded in Europe, North and Central America, and large parts of Asia.

These gains show that deforestation is not inevitable. When pressure on land falls, forests can return.

(This Data Insight was written by @MaxCRoser.)

In 2019, four years before Chandrayaan-3 captured global attention, Kaynes received a cryptic inquiry from ISRO. The requirements were specific yet vague: electronic systems capable of functioning in extreme temperature variations (-200°C to +125°C), surviving radiation levels that would fry normal circuits, and maintaining precision despite vibrations that could shake apart conventional assemblies. Most critically, these systems needed to be light—every gram mattered when fuel costs Rs 1 crore per kilogram to launch

Ramesh personally led the team responding to ISRO. This wasn't just another contract; it was a validation of everything Kaynes had built over three decades. The certifications accumulated, the zero-defect manufacturing culture, the harsh-environment expertise—all culminated in this moment. But ISRO's requirements pushed even Kaynes' capabilities. They needed electronics that could work in a vacuum, resist cosmic radiation, and operate autonomously 384,000 kilometers from Earth.

The development process was unlike any commercial project. ISRO engineers practically lived at Kaynes' facilities, reviewing every design decision, questioning every component choice, testing beyond normal breaking points. Failure modes that had 0.0001% probability in terrestrial applications became unacceptable risks in space. The company developed new testing protocols, invested in specialized equipment, and most importantly, created a dedicated team that thought in space-grade reliability

What Kaynes actually delivered for Chandrayaan-3 reveals the depth of capability required. The company provided critical electronic systems for both the Vikram lander and Pragyan rover—not just circuit boards but complete electronic subsystems that controlled navigation, communication, and scientific instruments.
The navigation electronics had to guide Vikram through the "15 minutes of terror"—the powered descent to the lunar surface, where split-second decisions determined success or failure. The systems processed data from multiple sensors, calculated trajectory corrections, and controlled thrusters, all while maintaining redundancy. If one circuit failed, backups had to activate instantly without ground intervention

For the Pragyan rover, Kaynes developed motor control electronics that would drive the six wheels across lunar regolith, camera systems that would capture and transmit images, and power management systems that would preserve battery life in the harsh lunar environment. Each system was manufactured in duplicate or triplicate—redundancy was a religion when repair was impossible

The manufacturing process itself was extraordinary. Clean rooms were upgraded to Class 100 standards. Components were individually tested and tracked. Each solder joint was X-rayed. Thermal cycling pushed assemblies through temperature ranges they'd never experience on Earth. Vibration testing simulated launch conditions that would destroy consumer electronics in seconds. Only after months of testing did ISRO accept the systems for integration

When Vikram touched down near the lunar south pole at 6:04 PM IST, making India the fourth nation to soft-land on the moon and the first to reach the south pole region, Ramesh watched from Kaynes' Mysore facility with hundreds of employees. Many had worked on the project without knowing its ultimate purpose—ISRO contracts came with strict confidentiality requirements

From the heart, truly ...

Yes, I called out Narayana, Neuland, and Laurus correctly. I am grateful for that. But please understand something important. Just because I got those right does not mean I will identify the next big winner.

Markets do not reward past accuracy with future certainty. Every new call is a fresh decision in a fresh environment. It is like a coin toss. Even if someone guessed correctly five times in a row, that streak tells you nothing about the very next flip.

I do not want admiration built on a streak. I want trust built on realism.

So please align your expectations. Appreciate the wins, yes. But do not assume they guarantee the next one. I will always do the work sincerely. I will always share with honesty. But certainty is something none of us truly have.

Let us stay grounded.
